schools.summary: Saul pictured as 'breathing out threatenings and slaughter' — a man wholly given to the church's destruction, breathing murder as his very air; the persecutor at the height of his fury as the narrative opens.
Calvin: the arrest of such a furious enemy, reclaimed against the whole bent of his mind, the most excellent mercy of God; the harder his rage, the greater the grace overcoming it.

Saul sought letters to arrest disciples 'of this way' — the first use of 'the Way' as a name for Christians (perhaps from John 14:6, before 'Christian,' Ellicott, Robertson); the Sanhedrin claiming jurisdiction over Jews everywhere (Barnes).
Calvin: the mention of women persecuted shows the special cruelty of Saul's zeal, sparing not even those whom war spares — a blot he later remembered with shame.

A sudden light from heaven like lightning (Robertson); Barnes argues strongly for a supernatural appearance of the glorified Christ in the Shekinah glory (the personal appearance of the Lord, 1 Corinthians 15:8); the three accounts (chs. 9, 22, 26) agree in essentials.
Calvin: a manifestation of Christ's divine majesty, that Saul might know he had to do with God himself.
Clarke suggests a divinely-used flash of lightning and thunder; Ellicott entertains a near-instantaneous thunderstorm not excluding the supernatural.

debates.question: Was the light a supernatural appearance of the glorified Christ or a divinely-used natural phenomenon?

sides.position: A supernatural appearance of the risen Christ in the Shekinah glory
A divinely-used flash of lightning/thunderstorm (not excluding the supernatural)

themes: suddenly there shined round about him
the appearance of the glorified Christ

schools.summary: Saul fell overpowered; the doubled name (Hebrew form) fixed his attention and claimed him as a Hebrew called by God (Barnes, Ellicott); 'why persecutest thou me' reveals the mystic union — Christ and his people one, persecuting them is persecuting him.
Calvin: the comfort that the Son of God is partner with his people in the cross, suffering with them, wounded through their side — and will avenge their wrongs.

themes: Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me
the mystic union of Christ and his church

schools.summary: 'Who art thou, Lord?' (Sir in dawning awe, Barnes, Robertson, or a higher sense, Clarke); 'I am Jesus' the despised Nazarene now revealed in glory (Barnes, Ellicott); 'kick against the pricks' absent from the best MSS here (inserted from 26:14), the proverb of resisting conscience's goads.
Calvin: the surrender of a man afraid and thrown down; the proverb applied to all who war against Christ, to their own double hurt — but the godly yield meekly to his goads.

debates.question: Are the words 'it is hard to kick against the pricks' original here?

sides.position: No — absent from the best MSS, inserted from Acts 26:14

themes: I am Jesus whom thou persecutest

schools.summary: 'What wilt thou have me to do?' (textually uncertain here, Robertson) the mark of true conversion — the prompt, entire surrender of the will to Christ (Barnes); even the most striking conversion needs the church's counsel.
Calvin: Christ sends Saul to Ananias, a common disciple, to adorn the ministry of the church — if the chief apostle was taught by a humble disciple, none may grudge God's ministers.

themes: Lord, what wilt thou have me to do

schools.summary: The apparent contradiction with 22:9 ('heard not the voice') resolved by the distinction between hearing a sound and understanding articulate words — they heard the sound but not the message (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson), as the crowd heard the voice as thunder (John 12:29).
Calvin: they heard the sound but discerned neither speaker nor words; the companions witnesses to the reality, but the message was Saul's alone.

themes: hearing a voice, but seeing no man

schools.summary: Saul's blindness the natural effect of the intense light (Barnes, 22:11), the cure miraculous (v.18); Ellicott traces possible permanent effects (large handwriting, the 'thorn in the flesh') and the spiritual significance — the guide of the blind made blind; led helpless by the hand (Robertson).
Calvin: the proud, learned persecutor blinded that he may see with his mind — the wise must become fools to attain true wisdom.

themes: led him by the hand into Damascus

schools.summary: Three days blind and fasting — the time of the new birth (Wesley); Barnes attributes the fast to overwhelming conviction of sin without assurance; Ellicott reads it as spiritual communion, perhaps the visions of 2 Corinthians 12.
Calvin: the fast part of the miracle and a sign of Saul's terror, as one dead tasting no food for three days.

themes: the pangs of the new birth

schools.summary: The Lord calls Ananias, a humble disciple (not an apostle), to restore Saul — his ready 'Behold, I am here, Lord' the obedience of a servant (like Samuel); the honour God puts on the ordinary ministry.
Chrysostom: God prepares both men by vision — Ananias to go and minister, Saul to profit by the ministry; the converging providence.

The Lord's precise direction (street, house, name, city — that Ananias might know it divine, Clarke); 'behold, he prayeth' the great sign of conversion — not the Pharisee's proud prayers but the penitent's, depending on God's mercy; real prayer the surest mark of the converted soul (Barnes).
Calvin: Saul continued in prayer those three days, the Lord deferring his comfort to kindle in him an earnest desire to pray.

The parallel vision preparing Saul to receive Ananias — the coincidence of the two visions displaying providence (Clarke); Ellicott answers the skeptical objection that this betrays the 'artistic historian' — the unity of design no stranger in God's work than in human skill.

themes: hath seen in a vision a man named Ananias
that he might receive his sight
the providence of God in conversion

schools.summary: Ananias's natural hesitation (the memory of Saul's fury, Barnes); the first use of 'saints' for the disciples — the consecrated, devout, a term passing from Ananias to Paul (Ellicott); Ananias saw the persecutor, Jesus the man transformed by grace (Robertson).
Calvin: the hesitation a weakness of faith mixed with obedience — a desire to obey tempered by fear, not withdrawing from Christ's command.

themes: I have heard by many of this man
how much evil he hath done to thy saints

schools.summary: Ananias presses the danger (Saul armed to bind the disciples, warned by letters from Jerusalem, Robertson); 'all that call on thy name' another term for Christians, from Joel via Peter (2:21) and Paul (Romans 10:13), passing from Ananias to Paul (Ellicott).
Calvin: to call on Christ's name is to worship him as God, placing all hope of salvation in him — a proof of his divinity, for invocation rests on sure confidence.

themes: authority to bind all that call on thy name

schools.summary: 'Go thy way' overrules Ananias's fear; 'a chosen vessel' (vessel of election) the first NT use of 'election,' prominent later in Paul (Romans 9, 11) — Ananias's language influencing the apostle (Ellicott); the threefold scope (Gentiles, kings, Israel) all fulfilled (Barnes).
Robertson/Wesley: Jesus chose Saul before Saul chose Jesus — unconditional election to a work; Calvin: Saul chosen for great and excellent things, all dependent on God's favour.

themes: to bear my name before the Gentiles and kings

schools.summary: Suffering part of Saul's call — 'how great things he must suffer,' fully accomplished (Barnes, 2 Corinthians 11); the strange change — the persecutor who inflicted suffering now suffers for the doctrine he persecuted (Clarke); the prospect welcome to the penitent (Ellicott).
Calvin: Christ accustoms Saul to bear the cross; no man fit to preach the gospel, against which the world is set, save one armed to suffer — the minister needing constancy and strength.

themes: how great things he must suffer

schools.summary: Ananias obeyed cheerfully (Gill) and greeted the former persecutor as 'Brother Saul' — the church receiving its enemy with love; he names the Lord Jesus who appeared (confirming the vision) and declares the twofold purpose: sight and the filling with the Spirit.
Cyril: the Spirit's mighty working changed Saul's blindness to newness of sight and sealed his soul, making him a chosen vessel.

themes: the Lord, even Jesus, that appeared unto thee
receive thy sight and be filled with the Holy Ghost

schools.summary: Sight restored, 'as it had been scales' falling — Barnes denies a literal removal (the cure miraculous though the blindness natural); Clarke/Ellicott read a literal incrustation peeling off, noting Luke's medical precision (cf. Tobit 11:13); baptism the indispensable condition of church admission (Ellicott).

debates.question: Did literal scales fall from Saul's eyes, or only an effect 'as if' scales?

sides.position: Only an effect 'as it had been' — no literal scales (the cure miraculous)
A literal incrustation/film peeling off (Luke's medical precision)

schools.summary: Saul strengthened by food after the fast, in fellowship with the Damascus disciples (the former persecutor now a brother); Luke omits the Arabian journey (Galatians 1:17), which Ellicott places here; Barnes draws the great evidential force of the conversion — Saul's unimpeachable testimony a strong proof of Christianity.

Saul straightway preached that Jesus (the better reading) is the Son of God — the immediate fruit and proof of his conversion (Barnes); the question being whether the crucified Jesus was that Christ and Son; Paul's lifelong platform of Jesus' full deity (Robertson).
Calvin: the swiftness of the fruit — the man who ran against Christ now fights as his standard-bearer to maintain his glory.

themes: that he is the Son of God

schools.summary: All amazed at Saul's change — 'destroyed' (made havoc, the threefold force: enemy, imprisoner, slayer, Clarke), the same verb Paul uses of himself (Galatians 1:13, Robertson); 'those who call on this name' again marking the worship of Christ.
Calvin: the amazement of those who knew his rage testifies that no cause but the hand of God could explain so sudden a change — the astonishment a proof of divine power.

themes: all that heard him were amazed
is not this he that destroyed them

schools.summary: Saul increased in strength — the spiritual power of Christ poured into him (Ellicott, Robertson, cf. Philippians 4:13); 'confounded' = confuted, throwing the Jews into confusion (Barnes); proving from Scripture that Jesus is the Christ.
Calvin: the victory came because Scripture was his sword; yet the Jews were confounded without being converted — overcome in argument but not yielding in heart.

themes: proving that this is very Christ

schools.summary: 'Many days' covering the Arabian journey and about three years (Galatians 1, Henry); the Jews, confounded in argument (v.22), resort to violence — the pattern of those who cannot overcome truth by reason; the plot fulfilling Christ's word that Saul 'must suffer.'

themes: the Jews took counsel to kill him

schools.summary: The plot known to Saul; the Jews with the ethnarch under Aretas (2 Corinthians 11:32, Ellicott) watched the gates day and night — the city sealed against the preacher, civil and Jewish authorities joined; the discovery the providence of God preserving Saul.

themes: their laying await was known of Saul
watched the gates day and night

schools.summary: The disciples let Saul down by the wall in a basket (2 Corinthians 11:33), like Rahab's spies and David (Robertson); Paul later refers to this undignified escape as an 'infirmity' he was content to boast of, a humbling memory (Ellicott).
Calvin: the escape lawful (avoiding the enemy, the law inclining to equity); the lesson of God's humbling — the apostle enforced to steal his life, acquainted betime with the cross.

themes: let him down by the wall in a basket

schools.summary: Saul (about three years after conversion, Galatians 1:18) tried to join the disciples, who feared and disbelieved him — natural, the memory of his violence, confidence a plant of slow growth (Barnes); Chrysostom harmonizes the apparent discrepancy with Galatians 1:17.

Calvin: Saul's hard entrance — rejected by his nation and distrusted by the church — borne patiently and humbly, desiring only to be numbered among Christ's disciples, the lowest place above all synagogue honors.

themes: he assayed to join himself to the disciples
they were all afraid of him
the hard entrance of the convert

schools.summary: Barnabas took Saul to the apostles (chiefly Peter and James, Galatians 1:18-19), vouching by three proofs — he saw the Lord, the Lord spoke to him, he preached boldly; Barnabas's readiness perhaps from an earlier friendship (Ellicott), his insight and courage to his credit (Robertson).
Chrysostom: the 'son of consolation,' a kind man, made himself easy of access and became the bridge by which the church received its former persecutor.

themes: the son of consolation vouches for Saul
a trusting friend to the convert

schools.summary: Saul received into free fellowship — 'coming in and going out' a Hebraism for intimate association (Calvin); the doors opened by Barnabas, Peter, and James, the disciples' fear vanished (Robertson); the visit lasting fifteen days (Galatians 1:18).

themes: coming in and going out at Jerusalem

schools.summary: Saul disputed boldly against the Hellenists — the very synagogues (Acts 6:9) where he opposed Stephen — seeking to undo the evil he wrought, taking Stephen's place and arguments (Ellicott, Robertson); the Hellenists offered him the same answer they gave Stephen: death.
Calvin: the irony — the persecutor now persecuted by his former allies, can abide nowhere, yet finds this suffering a better estate than his former triumph.

themes: he spake boldly in the name of the Lord Jesus
the second plot against his life

schools.summary: The brethren brought Saul to Caesarea and sent him to Tarsus — a proof of friendship (Ellicott) and the providence of God preserving him, as at Damascus; the return serving both safety and ministry in Cilicia (Galatians 1:21; Acts 15:41).

The churches had rest, edified, walking in the fear of the Lord and the comfort of the Spirit, multiplied; the rest from Saul's removal and especially the providential diversion of the Jews by Caligula's crisis (the statue in the temple, Barnes, Clarke, Lardner).

themes: walking in the fear of the Lord
the comfort of the Holy Ghost
the season of peace and growth

schools.summary: Peter, in a season of peace, went throughout the churches to the saints at Lydda — diligent, humble pastoral visitation (Henry); the peace (v.31) freeing him to strengthen the believers, opening the account of Lydda and Joppa that prepares for Cornelius (ch. 10).
Chrysostom: Peter like a commander inspecting his ranks, going about to strengthen what needed his presence.

themes: as Peter passed throughout all quarters

schools.summary: Aeneas, a Greek name (probably a Hellenistic Jew, Barnes, Robertson), bedridden eight years with palsy — a long, hopeless affliction noted by Luke the physician; the chronic disease magnifying the miracle to come, beyond natural cure.

Peter disclaims his own power — 'Jesus Christ maketh thee whole,' ascribing the cure wholly to Christ (Barnes, Clarke); the assonance (Iesus iatai se) testifying Jesus is the great Healer (Ellicott); 'make thy bed' the proof of the cure.
Calvin: Peter spoke with certainty (knowing God's will) and as mere minister, extolling the name of Christ alone.

themes: the name of Jesus the Healer

schools.summary: All at Lydda and Sharon (the fertile coastal plain, a district, Barnes, Robertson) saw the healed Aeneas and turned to the Lord — 'all' meaning a great part (Calvin); the miracle leading to wide conversion.
Calvin: the proper end of miracles is to bring men to embrace Christ — those who look only at the wonder, not at conversion, corrupt the miracle.

themes: all at Lydda and Saron saw him
conversion the end of the sign

schools.summary: Dorcas (Tabitha, 'gazelle') 'full of good works and almsdeeds' — devoted to charity, especially the poor widows (Henry); at the head of a sisterhood of mercy, making garments with her own hands (Ellicott); the model of practical Christian charity.
Chrysostom: the providence in her name — as active and watchful in good works as the swift gazelle she was named for.

themes: a disciple named Tabitha, called Dorcas
full of good works and almsdeeds

schools.summary: Dorcas died, her body washed (the ancient custom, Clarke) and laid in an upper chamber — the careful preparation establishing the reality of death; the upper chamber implying a delay and perhaps a hope, the news of Aeneas's healing nearby raising expectation (Ellicott).
Calvin: the washing and upper chamber signs of some hope of recovery; the ancient washing of the dead a sign of the hope of the resurrection.

themes: laid her in an upper chamber

schools.summary: The disciples, hearing Peter was nearby at Lydda (nine miles), sent two men urging him not to delay; their motive cautiously read — comfort or a modest hope (the apostles having raised no one yet, Barnes); possibly sent before she died (Clarke, Robertson).
Calvin: a humble hope — not that Dorcas be made immortal, but that her useful life be prolonged for the church's good.

themes: they sent unto him two men
that he would not delay to come

schools.summary: Peter came to the upper chamber where the widows wept and showed the garments Dorcas made — a heart-breaking scene (Robertson); the widows, objects of the church's organized charity and fellow-workers with her (Ellicott), the garments the visible memorial of her good works.
Calvin: God raised Dorcas because he pitied the poor and would, by relieving their need, establish their faith in the gospel.

themes: all the widows stood by him weeping
the coats and garments which Dorcas made
the memorial of her good works

schools.summary: Peter put them out (as Christ with Jairus's daughter, Mark 5:40), kneeled, prayed, and said 'Tabitha, arise' — the raising by the prayer of faith, not the utterance of a name, in dependence on Christ (Barnes, Ellicott); the words almost the Talitha cumi of Christ (Mark 5:41); echoing Elijah and Elisha (Robertson).
Calvin: Peter withdrew to pray, confessing the matter was not in his own hand but God's — unlike Christ who raised by his own authority.

themes: Peter put them all forth, and prayed
raising by the prayer of faith

schools.summary: Peter gave Dorcas his hand, lifted her up, and presented her alive (as Christ with Jairus's daughter and Elijah with the widow's son, Chrysostom, Barnes) — life given by the word, strength by the hand; the joy of those who received back their benefactress.
Calvin: she was raised for others' sake and the glory of Christ — though called from blessed rest, made a more excellent instrument of God's goodness.

themes: he gave her his hand, and lifted her up
the joy of the saints and widows
raised for the glory of Christ

schools.summary: The raising of Dorcas known throughout Joppa, leading many to believe — the first apostolic raising of the dead, like the raising of Lazarus (John 12:11, Barnes); the report of so great a wonder could not be hidden.

Calvin: the manifold fruit — the poor comforted, the matron restored, the church's loss repaired, many called to faith; the faith terminating not on Peter but on the Lord, to whom Peter directed them.

themes: it was known throughout all Joppa

schools.summary: Peter tarried at Joppa with Simon a tanner — a trade the Jews despised as unclean; his lodging there marking his humility and a providential preparation, for here the messengers from Cornelius would find him and the vision come (ch. 10).
Chrysostom: Peter's unassuming conduct — lodging not with the distinguished but with a tanner, leading men to humility, neither shaming the lowly nor elating the great.

themes: he tarried many days in Joppa
